text: From BBC Learning English,

this is Learning English from the News,
our podcast about the news headlines.
In this programme,
'We have to have it' -

that's what President Trump is saying
about Greenland.
Hello I'm Pippa.
And I'm Phil.

In this programme we look
at one big news story

and the vocabulary in the headlines
that will help you understand it.

You can find all the vocabulary
and headlines

from this episode as well
as a worksheet on our website,
bbclearningenglish.com.
So, let's hear more about this story.

'One way or another
we're going to have Greenland' -

those are the words
of US President Donald Trump,

who has said that the US
will take control of Greenland.

Greenland is a large island in
between the Atlantic and Arctic oceans,
which is ruled by Denmark.

Many people in Greenland feel that
the territory should become independent.

At the moment
it has its own government,

but Denmark controls its foreign,
defence and security policy.

The territory's strategic position
in the Arctic has led

to previous attempts by the US
to purchase it.

The US military currently has a base
on the island,

and President Trump
has stated concerns

that the territory could
be taken over by Russia or China.

These fears
have been dismissed by Denmark.

These latest comments by
President Trump have led to criticism

from many European countries,
who have said that they will support

Denmark's attempt to keep the territory
as part of the Kingdom of Denmark.

President Trump has responded
to these criticisms

by threatening to impose taxes on
imports, otherwise known as tariffs,

on a number of European countries
who are allies of the US.
Let's have our first headline.

This is from the Financial Times,
a British financial newspaper.
Greenland is ground zero for Arctic
'Great Game'.

That headline again
from the Financial Times.
Greenland is ground zero for Arctic
'Great Game'.

This headline is
about the importance of Greenland

to powerful countries such as the US,
Russia and China.

The 'Great game' means the competition
between powerful countries.

However, we're going
to land the expression ground zero.

The original literal meaning
of the expression is the location

directly under a large explosion,
especially a nuclear explosion.

It's also often been used to refer to
the location of the terrorist attack

on New York's World Trade Centre
in September 2001.

Ground zero is also used to talk
about the areas

of most damage caused by
natural disasters.

For example, we can talk about
the ground zero of hurricane damage.
As a metaphor,
we can use ground zero to talk

about where
something important begins to happen.

Greenland could be the start
of a bigger dispute or conflict.
We've had ground zero,

the location where
something important starts happening.

For example, this street is ground zero
for the city's latest crime wave.
It all started here.
This is Learning English

from the News, our podcast
about the news headlines.

We're talking
about President Trump's comments
about taking control of Greenland.

President Trump has said that the US
needs to control Greenland to stop

Russia or China taking control
of the island.

The United States
and Denmark are both Nato countries.

Nato is a military alliance
that was formed during the Cold War

to protect European countries
from the Soviet Union.

It's been suggested that conflict
between Nato allies could lead
to the end of the alliance.

And that's what
our next headline is about.

This headline is from CNN,
an American news organisation.

Why Trump's split with Europe
over Greenland
is a double edged sword for Russia.

That headline again from CNN,
an American news organisation.

Why Trump's split with Europe over
Greenland is a double edged sword
for Russia.

We're going to learn the expression
a double edged sword.

This is a metaphor used
to describe a situation that has

both positive and negative
consequences for someone.
This article talks about how,

on the one hand, Russia is likely
to see the split between the US

and its Nato allies
as something positive.
The article then says that,

on the other hand, Russia will see
greater American influence in the Arctic
as something negative.

We often use double edged sword for
things that appear positive at first,

but turn out
to also have negative consequences.

The spread of AI could be seen
as a double edged sword.

On the one hand, it can make
some tasks much easier for us.

But on the other hand, many things
produced by AI are of low quality.

A different policy
or approach to doing something

could also turn out to be
a double edged sword.
A new job that pays you more

but is much more stressful
could be a double edged sword.
We've had double edged sword,

something that has both positive
and negative consequences.

For example, having a pool is
a double edged sword.

It's great fun to swim in,
but you have to clean it every day.

This is Learning English from the News,
our podcast about the news headlines.
Today, we've been talking

about Donald Trump's intention
to have control over Greenland.

Following President Trump's comments
about taking control of Greenland,

a number of European countries
have stated their opposition

and also sent small numbers of troops
to the territory.

In response,
Trump has threatened to impose

increased trade tariffs, taxes paid
on products imported into the US.
And here's our next headline.

This is from CNBC, an American
business news organisation.

Greenland PM issues defiant rebuke
over Trump's tariff threats:
'We will not be pressured'.
That headline again from CNBC.

Greenland PM issues defiant rebuke
over Trump's tariff threats:
'We will not be pressured'.
This headline is saying

that the Prime Minister of Greenland,
Jens-Frederik Nielsen,

has said that his government will
not give in to pressure from the US.
We're going to learn the word rebuke.
A rebuke is a strong criticism.
The author of this headline is saying

that Nielsen
is strongly criticising Trump.
Rebuke is quite a formal word.

It's often seen in reports
on criticism by and of politicians,

but is less common
in everyday speech.

While the word is formal, the act of
rebuking someone isn't necessarily.

It can just mean strongly disagreeing
or disapproving.
Here, rebuke is a noun,

but it can also be used
as a verb to rebuke someone.
We've had rebuke, a strong criticism.

For example,
the manager rebuked her staff
after their behaviour at the party.

That's it for this episode
of Learning English from the News.

We'll be back next week
with another news story.

And if you want to practise
what you've learned,

go to our website to see more example
sentences and try our free worksheet.
That's bbclearningenglish.com.

And don't forget to follow us
on social media.
Find us @bbclearningenglish.

Bye for now.
Bye.
